_meta.note: delta_v1 format — editorial covers what_held / what_changed / what_new. Numbers come from system_model.json + annotations. Do not manually duplicate data from those files here.

editorial.issue_title: Three Held, One Confirmed, Two Revealed
editorial.hero_narrative: One month of new data. Three signals from Issue #1 are now confirmed structural. One that was uncertain is now explained. And the full merged series reveals two patterns that a single-period file can never show. This is what a credit data series does when you read it across time — not a bulletin, a model.

what_held.curr_stat: +359% over 24 months across three March data points
what_held.note: The merged series adds Mar 2024 and Mar 2025 data points that the Jan 2026 file never contained. The trajectory is steeper and more consistent than a single-period snapshot suggested. The structural shift from unsecured to gold-backed household borrowing is now unambiguous.

what_held.curr_stat: +31.2% YoY confirmed + PSL Micro & Small +25.6% FY independently corroborating
what_held.note: Two independent series — total MSME credit and PSL Micro & Small — pointing the same direction. This removes the single-series concern. The UDYAM + GST formalisation wave has crossed the underwriting threshold at scale.

what_held.prev_stat: +35.9% YoY — Jan 2026, three consecutive periods of acceleration
what_held.curr_stat: +62% across three February data points (Feb 2024, Feb 2025, Feb 2026)
what_held.note: The Feb data points extend the series to a clean 24-month arc. The acceleration is not a Jan spike — the same trajectory appears in the February cross-section. PLI drawdowns are in progress.

what_changed.prev_read: Issue #1 flagged the +37.9% surge as suspicious after two flat periods. Three possible explanations were offered: PMAY acceleration, reclassification, or genuine new origination. Left as uncertain.
what_changed.curr_read: The merged gaps analysis confirms reclassification. The Oct 2024 RBI revision raised PSL housing loan ceilings (metro: ₹35L → ₹45L, non-metro: ₹25L → ₹35L). Loans already on bank books that previously exceeded the old limits now qualify as PSL. The ₹2.87L Cr appearing as credit growth is existing exposure being relabelled, not new lending.
what_changed.implication: PSL achievement figures for FY26 housing are not comparable to prior years. Banks citing PSL compliance gains from this number should disclose the classification change. The affordable housing opportunity is real but smaller than the +38% headline implies.

what_new.signal: FY26 accelerating above FY25 — only visible with the merged series
what_new.body: Total bank credit FY26 is tracking at 13.8% (Feb 2026 vs Mar 2025) against FY25's full-year 11.0%. This comparison requires both the Jan 2026 and Feb 2026 data points alongside the March bases — impossible from any single SIBC file. The acceleration is not base effect: the trajectory is consistent across both periods.
what_new.implication: Any portfolio that grew less than 13% in FY26 to February has lost market share. The system is in genuine expansion, not recovery.

what_new.signal: Infrastructure credit decelerating — next capex wave not yet at drawdown scale
what_new.body: Infrastructure is 32.5% of all industry credit and growing at the slowest rate in the dataset. The FY22-24 capex wave projects are now in operations, repaying rather than drawing down. The next wave — green hydrogen, data centres, renewable energy storage — has not yet reached credit drawdown scale. The approval-to-drawdown gap is typically 18-24 months.
what_new.implication: Corporate banks with heavy infrastructure exposure face a structural growth headwind for 12-18 months. The window to build capability for the next capex wave is now.

bullets: Gold loan growth rate — does +359% sustain through Mar 2026, or do we see the first signs of saturation as early NBFC movers hit accessible TAM limits?
MSME base effect — Mar 2026 will be the first March-to-March comparison with formalisation baked into both sides. Watch whether +31% compresses as the base normalises.
PSL Housing — does the reclassification-driven +38% persist, or does growth normalise as the re-eligible loan pool is exhausted?
Infrastructure sub-sectors — first signs of the next capex wave: renewable energy project finance, data centre construction lending. Watch for any Infrastructure sub-sector breaking above +10% FY.
